segments.s354-g33: چو آمد is an archaic/poetic temporal clause meaning “when he came” (چو = when + آمد = past ‘came’). به نزدیک + NP is a prepositional phrase ‘near / toward the great king’ with به + نزدیک preceding the noun, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s354-g34: بدید is simple past 3rd‑person ‘he saw’. آن is the demonstrative determiner ‘that/the’. در و بارگاه بزرگ is a coordinated object ‘the gate and the great palace’; word order here places the verb before its noun phrase, a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s354-g35: چو آگاهی آمد is another temporal clause ‘when information/news came’. به + سالار بار is a prepositional phrase ‘to the leader/commander of the baggage/escort’ (سالار = commander; بار here refers to load/baggage, a military/escort context).
segments.s354-g36: خرامان is an adverbial participle meaning ‘with a stately/proud gait’; بیامد is past ‘came’. بر شهریار is the directional prepositional phrase ‘toward the king’ (بر = toward/onto + شهریار = king).
segments.s354-g37: که introduces a subordinate clause here ‘for/that’; پیر جهاندیده‌ای is a descriptive NP ‘an old, world‑wise one’ (پیر = old/sage; جهاندیده‌ای = worldly‑wise). بر درست is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘truly/rightly’ (درست = correct; بر functions as an intensifier here).
segments.s354-g38: همانا is an emphatic particle ‘verily’ or ‘indeed’. فرستاده is the noun ‘messenger’ and قیصرست is a fused form of noun + copula (قیصر + ست/=است) meaning ‘is Caesar’s / is of Caesar’ — an archaic poetic way to assert identity.
segments.s354-g39: سوارست is a predicative compound meaning ‘he was mounted / a horseman’ (سوار + است). با او marks accompaniment ‘with him’. بسی is an intensive adverb ‘very/much’ modifying نامدار ‘renowned’ (here: ‘many/very renowned’), so the phrase describes his distinguished retinue.
segments.s354-g40: همی is a present/habitual marker here (‘continually/still’). راه جوید is present‑tense ‘he seeks/seeks the way’ (جوید = 3rd‑person singular present). بر شهریار is the directional phrase ‘to/toward the king’, showing the object/goal of his seeking.
segments.s354-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning “when.” بشنید is simple past “he heard.” بنشست is past “he sat down.” The prepositional phrase بر تخت عاج places the action “on the ivory throne” (بر + noun, with عاج qualifying تخت).
segments.s354-g42: بسر here functions as an archaic/poetic locative equivalent to ‘بر سر’ (on the head); this specific lemma list does not supply that sense. بر نهاد is a compound where نهاد is past “placed/put” and بر introduces the target. آن is the demonstrative “that,” and دل‌افروز qualifies تاج as “heart‑cheering crown.”
segments.s354-g43: بزرگان ایران is a noun phrase “the great ones of Iran” (noun + genitive). همه is the distributive “all/everyone.” پیش تخت is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the presence of / before the throne.” Word order is NP + locative phrase.
segments.s354-g44: نشستند is intended as the past plural ‘they sat,’ but the provided lemma/senses list is incorrect for this form, so no matching sense is available. شادان دل و نیکبخت are predicative/adjectival phrases describing their state: “joyful of heart and fortunate.”
segments.s354-g45: بفرمود is an honorific/simple past ‘he commanded/said (with polite form). تا here introduces the result or consequence (‘so/thereupon’). پرده برداشتند is a past plural clause ‘they removed/lifted the curtain.’
segments.s354-g46: فرستاده را marks the direct object with را: “the messenger.” شاد describes the manner (“happy/cheerful”), and بگذاشتند here means ‘they let (him) go / sent him off’ (the second sense, “they left/let go”).
segments.s354-g47: چو آمد is a temporal clause “when he came.” به نزدیک تختش places the arrival spatially “near his throne.” فراز is an adjectival/heightive qualifier meaning “on high/lofty,” modifying the sense of the throne (poetic hauteur).
segments.s354-g48: بر او آفرین کرد is an idiom where آفرین functions as an acclamation/noun “praise” and کرد is the verb “made/did,” so together “he praised him” or “gave him acclaim.” و connects the clauses. بردش here is the verb ‘he took/led him,’ but the provided sense list for that lemma does not include this transitive past sense, so no matching index is available. نماز is the object “prayer.”
segments.s354-g81: فرستاده گفت is a reporting clause: فرستاده (subject) + گفت (past tense 'said'). The following vocative ای سخنگوی شاه is the addressee introduced directly after گفت; ای is the archaic/exclamatory vocative particle 'O', سخنگوی شاه is the noun phrase 'spokesman of the king'. Word order here places the reporter before the speech and the vocative after the verb.
segments.s354-g82: به مرز خزر is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the Khazar frontier' (به + noun). من شدم باژخواه is a nominal predicate with subject pronoun من before the past verb شدم ('I became' / 'I came to be'), followed by the predicative noun باژخواه 'toll‑seeker, extortioner' — i.e. 'I took on the role of a toll‑collector.' The verbless-copula feel is conveyed by شدم.
segments.s354-g83: به پیغمبری is 'in/for prophethood' (به + deverbal/nominal form پیغمبری). رنج بردم is a fixed verbal phrase 'I bore/underwent suffering' (رنج + بردم, past of بردن used here as 'to endure'); بسی is an adverb 'much/greatly' modifying the verb. The clause expresses speaker's complaint about having endured hardship in that role.
segments.s354-g84: نپرسید is past‑tense negative third‑person 'did not ask' (ن- prefix + پرسید). زین باره here means 'about this matter/affair' (زین = 'this', باره = 'matter/instance') and هرگز کسی is the negative existential focus 'never did anyone' (هرگز 'never' + کسی 'anyone'); together they form 'never did anyone ask about this.'
segments.s354-g85: ولیکن is a formal/poetic adversative 'but'. مرا is the object 'me' (here orthographically split as م + را in the word list: م = me, را = object marker). شاه is the agent 'the king' and زان‌سان نواخت is a resultative phrase 'struck/treated (me) thus' (زان‌سان = 'so/that way', نواخت = past of نواختن used here as 'struck/treated').
segments.s354-g86: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'that/so that'. گردن به کژی is a figurative phrase 'neck toward crookedness' (گردن = 'neck', به = preposition, کژی = 'crookedness/wrongness') and نباید فراخت is a negative modal construction: نباید = 'one must not/should not' and فراخت is an archaic/poetic verb here meaning 'to bow/bend (the neck)'; together: 'that one should not bend the neck to crookedness.'
segments.s354-g87: سواری به نزدیک او آمدست is a simple clause: سواری 'a rider/horseman' + به نزدیک 'near' + او 'him' + آمدست (poetic perfect 'has come' = 'came'). The verb آمدست is an older/poetic perfect form used here instead of آمد or آمدن.
segments.s354-g88: که starts a relative clause 'who/that'. از بیشه‌ها شیر گیرد literally 'takes/seizes a lion from the thickets into (his) hand': از = 'from', بیشه‌ها = plural 'thickets/groves', شیر here is 'lion' (poetic motif), گیرد is present/subjunctive/relative verb form of گرفتن 'to seize/take' used here as a relative present ('who seizes' or 'who had seized'), and به دست 'in (his) hand' completes the action. The whole clause describes the horseman as one who has seized a lion from the thicket.
segments.s354-g1: برین is a fused demonstrative phrase (بر + این) meaning 'on/after this'; نیز means 'also/too'. بگذشت is a past-tense impersonal verb 'passed' (of time) and چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. سپهر is the grammatical subject 'the sky/heavens' placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s354-g2: به دل در همی داشت is an idiomatic phrase: به دل ('in the heart') + در (emphatic 'inside') + همی داشت, where همی is a classical continuous/respective particle and داشت here means 'held/kept' (past progressive sense 'was holding, was inwardly troubled'). و ننمود چهر is coordinate: ننمود is negative past 'did not show/display' and چهر is 'face/visage' (here 'the face of heaven' i.e. the sun/fortune).
segments.s354-g3: بگشتاسپ is the speaker's name (Vishtaspa). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech); آن زمان 'then/at that time' is a time adverbial, and جنگجوی is the vocative 'O warrior' or the addressee. Word order places the verb before the time phrase and vocative for poetic effect.
segments.s354-g4: که introduces the content clause. تا زنده‌ای is a temporal/conditional phrase 'as long as you are alive' (زنده‌ای = 'you are alive' / 'while you live'). زین جهان is 'from/of this world' (z- + این = 'this' in archaic use) and بهر جوی is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'seek for/seek (something)'; جوی is the noun/gerund 'seeking' or 'search' and بهر 'for/toward' marks purpose.
segments.s354-g5: براندیش is an imperative/hortative verb 'consider/ponder'. با این سخن 'with these words' means 'on this speech' and با خرد 'with wisdom' is an adverbial phrase modifying the imperative. The order places the instrument/attitude phrases after the verb in the classical poetic style.
segments.s354-g6: که introduces an explanatory clause: اندیشه اندر سخن = 'thought within speech' (اندر = 'in/within'). به خورد is idiomatic and here metaphorical: خورد is literally 'to eat', but in classical usage means 'to be taken/received' or 'to befit/become' — i.e. the thought in words is something to be taken in. The sequence marks a reason for the preceding imperative.
segments.s354-g7: به ایران is the destination 'to Iran'. فرستم is a present/future-tense first-person verb 'I send / I will send'. فرستاده‌ای is the indefinite object 'an envoy/a sent one' (the -ای ending here gives an indefinite/characterizing sense). Word order is verb followed by its object for emphasis.
segments.s354-g8: A string of epithets modifying the envoy: جهاندیده 'worldly-wise, experienced', پاک 'pure', و آزاده‌ای 'and a free/noble one' (آزاده‌ای here serves as an adjectival noun with the -ای ending). The adjectives are coordinated with و and all describe the intended qualities of the messenger.
segments.s354-g89: This clause is verb-final Persian: the verb بخندد (he laughs / laughs at) comes before the temporal phrase روز رزم (the day of battle) for poetic emphasis. به marks the object/target ('at' or 'toward') and مردان is the object 'men' or 'heroes'. همی is a continuous/adverbial particle 'still/continually' or here emphasizing habitual action. Word order: [prepositional object] + [verb] + [adverb] + [temporal phrase]; the verb governs the whole clause.
segments.s354-g90: This short nominal phrase lacks an explicit verb and is linked to the previous clause (the verb from the first clause is understood to extend here). هم = 'also/too' ties the line to the prior clause. از جامه + می‌به forms a genitive/descritive phrase 'a garment of/coloured like mi‑beh' (mi‑beh is an archaic/poetic term related to wine/quince, here treated as a colour or type of robe). هنگام بزم is a temporal phrase 'at the time of the feast'. The overall sense: 'and also, at the feast, [he has] a wine‑coloured garment.'
segments.s354-g91: This is a coordinated series of prepositional phrases introduced by به 'at/in': به بزم (at the feast), و به رزم (and in battle), و به‌روز شکار (and on the day of the hunt). The repetition of به is emphatic/poetic, listing the contexts in which the following statement holds.
segments.s354-g92: جهان‌بین here functions as a general subject ('the world' / 'those who behold the world') and ندیدست is an archaic contracted perfect negative meaning 'has not been seen' (poetic form of نَـدیده است). چون = 'like/as', introducing the comparison, and او سوار = 'a rider like him' (او = 'him', سوار = 'rider/horseman'). Word order is Subject + Verb + Comparative phrase, with the verb in the past/perfect sense.
segments.s354-g93: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him'. داد is the simple past 'gave'. پرمایه‌تر is the comparative form of پرمایه ('rich, substantial'), meaning 'more bountiful/wealthy'. دخترش is 'his daughter' with the possessive suffix -ش. The sequence is standard S-V-O with the indirect object (to him) fronted for emphasis.
segments.s354-g94: This is a relative clause introduced by که 'who/that' modifying دخترش. بودی is the past tense form of بودن 'was' (poetic/past; here third‑person singular). گرامی‌تر is the comparative 'more honored/precious'. از marks the standard comparative 'than', and افسرش is 'his afsar' with possessive suffix; in this older register افسر usually means 'helmet' (not modern 'officer'). The clause reads 'who was more precious than his helmet.'
segments.s354-g95: نشانی here means 'sign/token/fame'; شدست is a poetic contraction of شده است 'has become' (perfective auxiliary). او is the subject 'he'. به روم اندرون is an adverbial phrase 'within Rome' or 'into the interior of Rome' (به + place + اندرون). The clause says he has become a notable sign/figure inside Rome.
segments.s354-g96: چو = 'as/like', introducing a simile: نر اژدها = 'a male dragon' (narrowly, 'male' + 'dragon/serpent'). شد is 'became'. به چنگش is a prepositional phrase 'into/under his claw/grip' (چنگش = his claw/grip), and زبون here is adjectival/noun meaning 'the vanquished / abject one(s)' (sense 'hapless/abject'), so the whole clause means 'when he became like a male dragon, the defeated fell into his grip.'
segments.s354-g49: This phrase places the noun phrase 'پیام گرانمایه' ('a precious message') before the indirect object 'قیصر' and the verb 'بداد' (an archaic past form meaning 'gave'/'delivered'), a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi. Grammatically it functions as '[He] delivered the Caesar the precious message' with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.s354-g50: چنان چون is an emphatic pair meaning 'such/as' and introduces a comparison or manner. بباید is an impersonal/third-person present of بایستن meaning 'it ought/should be', so the clause reads 'as it ought to be'. به + آیین و داد is a prepositional phrase 'according to ritual/custom (آیین) and justice (داد)', where داد is a noun 'justice'.
segments.s354-g51: غمی شد is a two-word predicate 'became sorrow' (شد = 'became'). ز + گفتار او is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of his words'. شهریار at the end is the subject in topical position: 'The king became sorrowful because of his words.'
segments.s354-g52: برآشفت is a past form meaning 'he was angered/grew upset'. با + گردش روزگار is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by/with the turning (گردش) of روزگار' — روزگار here refers to fate or the course of time, so the clause means 'he grew angry at the vicissitudes of fate.'
segments.s354-g53: گرانمایه جایی is a noun phrase 'a precious/esteemed place' (adjective before noun in poetical ordering). بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned/arranged', so the clause means 'they prepared/ornamented a splendid place.'
segments.s354-g54: فرستاده را uses the object marker را to mark 'the envoy' as the direct object. شاد is an adjectival/adverbial modifier 'glad/happily'. بنشاستند is an archaic past plural causative/denominative form meaning 'they caused to sit' or 'they seated (him)'. The full meaning: 'they seated the envoy happily.'
segments.s354-g55: فرستاد is a past-verb form 'they sent/dispatched'. زربفت گستردنی is a noun phrase where زربفت 'gold-brocade' is qualified by گستردنی ('for spreading/laying out'), so the phrase names one class of gifts: brocade to be spread/laid over surfaces.
segments.s354-g56: ز پوشیدنی و هم از خوردنی uses two partitive prepositions (ز and از) in a parallel list. پوشیدنی ('things to wear' / garments) and خوردنی ('things to eat' / edibles) are gerundial/nominal adjectives naming more categories of gifts; و هم adds 'and also'. The construction lists 'garments and also food'.
segments.s354-g73: نبود is a past negative copula meaning 'was not' or 'did not exist' (here used existentially). Word order is verb first followed by the topic/object phrase (نبود این هنرها) — literally 'was not these arts' — and the location phrase به روم اندرون ('in Rome's inner [court]') follows to mark where they did not exist.
segments.s354-g74: بدی here names a defect/poor quality (a noun 'badness' or 'weakness') and is predicated of قیصر; the clause is elliptical (no separate copula needed because past-tense sense is implied). از پیش شاهان زبون is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the kings' with زبون functioning as a predicate adjective 'abject/pliant'.
segments.s354-g75: کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb starting the clause. او is the third-person pronoun 'he' (subject). بهر plus کشوری marks purpose/goal ('to/for a district' or 'for a province') and باژخواه is the noun 'extortioner/toll‑collector' — this whole nominal phrase is the object/goal of the verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s354-g76: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent' (the verb that takes the noun phrase from the previous segment as its object). و is the coordinating 'and' connecting the following action. بر is the locative/prepositional 'on/upon', ماه is a substantive here (literal 'moon' in the lemma list, but in poetic usage could denote a place or be part of a locative expression), بنهاد is past 'he placed/founded' and گاه is the noun 'place/station' receiving the action — together 'and he placed/set a station/place upon ...'.
segments.s354-g77: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. الیاس is the proper name 'Elias' and is marked by the object/topic marker را to highlight him as the topical element. کو here functions as a relative/locative 'who/where' introducing the subordinate locative clause 'who was at the Khazar border' (به مرز خزر).
segments.s354-g78: گوی بود is a common epic formula: گوی 'it is said / it was said' (or 'one says') + بود 'was' gives an indirect-reportive sense ('it was said that... / he was said to be'). با introduces accompanying attributes 'with' and is followed by فر 'royal glory' and the coordinating و plus پرخاشخر describing him as quarrelsome/aggressive — together describing Elias's reputed qualities.
segments.s354-g79: بگیرد and ببندد are present/habitual-prefixed verb forms (ب‑ + root) used in narrative to express repeated or characteristic actions: 'seizes' / 'binds' (here translated 'would seize and bind' to capture the iterative sense). همی is an adverbial particle reinforcing continuity/regularity ('continually/always'). با سپاه marks the instrument/force 'with an army'.
segments.s354-g80: بدین is the fused prepositional/demonstrative 'by/with this' (formally ב + دین in the segmentation here; in context it functions as 'by this/with this'). باژخواهش is the extortioner with a possessive/relational suffix ('his extortioner' / 'this extortioner' depending on context), and که introduces a relative clause 'who/that' modifying that extortioner. بنمود is past 'showed/revealed' and راه is the direct object 'the way/route' — 'by this extortioner who showed the way.'
segments.s354-g25: وگرنه = 'otherwise' (sentence adverb). م(را) is the object clitic 'me' (here written split as م + را); the explicit verb is omitted (common in poetry) so this phrase means 'against me, with a powerful army'. با + سپاهی گران is a prepositional phrase 'with a heavy/powerful army' where سپاهی (soldiers/army) is qualified by the adjective گران 'heavy/strong'.
segments.s354-g26: هم = an intensifying 'even' or 'also' here ('even'). از روم = 'from Rum' (Rum = Byzantium/lands of the Romans). وز is actually the poetic contraction of و + ز ('and from') before دشت; the annotation lists senses that don't match this conjunctive use, so treat it as the conjunction+preposition. دشت نیزه‌وران = 'the plain (dast) of spearmen' where نیزه‌وران means 'spearmen' or 'lancers'.
segments.s354-g27: نگه کن is an imperative 'look!/watch!'. که introduces a subordinate clause here ('that' / 'for'), followed by برخیزد which is third-person singular present/subjunctive 'may rise' or 'will rise' (the optative/subjunctive form used to report a potential event). از دشت غو = 'an uproar from the plain' (غو 'uproar, tumult') with the prepositional phrase placed before the head noun for poetic effect.
segments.s354-g28: فرخ‌زاد is a proper name. پیروزشان is پیروز + شان 'their victorious (one)' or 'their victor' — here an epithet applied to the leader. پیش رو is a compound meaning 'in front' / 'at the head' (پیش 'before/in front of', رو functioning as part of the locative expression). The phrase places Farkhzād as the leader at the fore of the rising tumult.
segments.s354-g29: همه بومتان = 'all your land/homeland' (همه 'all', بوم 'land, homeland' + possessive تان). پاک here functions as an intensifier 'utterly/completely' rather than 'pure'. ویران کنم is the verb phrase 'I will make ruin / I will lay waste' (کنم = 1st-person present/subjunctive used to express the speaker's resolve).
segments.s354-g30: ز ایران is a poetic 'from/in Iran' marking the domain affected. به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by the sword' (به + instrument). بیران کنم = 'I will make (it) desolate' or 'I will turn (it) into a wilderness' (بیران here denotes desolation), with کنم again the 1st-person verb of action.
segments.s354-g31: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause 'a messenger came'. به کردار باد is a simile 'in the manner/behavior of the wind' (به کردار = 'in the manner of', کردار 'conduct/way', باد 'wind') — i.e., he arrived swiftly like the wind.
segments.s354-g32: This is a pair of nominal predicates describing the messenger. سرش پر خرد = 'his head full of wisdom' (سر + possessive ش, پر 'full (of)', خرد 'wisdom'). بد دلش پر ز داد = 'his stern/harsh heart full of justice' — بد modifies دلش ('ill/stern-hearted'), پر ز = 'full of' with ز introducing the content, داد = 'justice'. The construction uses 'پر' + ز + noun to express 'full of X.'
segments.s354-g17: Word-for-word order: 'یکی نامور بود' = 'there was a certain renowned (one)'; 'قالوس نام' is postpositive, literally 'Qalous named', i.e. 'named Qalous'. 'بود' is past-tense 'was'. 'یکی' here functions as an indefinite 'a certain'.
segments.s354-g18: This is a list of qualities describing the same subject. 'خردمند' is a predicative adjective 'wise'. 'و با دانش' uses 'با' ('with') to indicate possession/association of 'دانش' ('knowledge'). 'و رای و کام' continues the listing: 'رای' here means 'counsel/judgment' and 'کام' 'desire/wish'. The verb from the previous segment ('بود') supplies the verbal backbone for this description.
segments.s354-g19: 'بخواند' is a jussive/optative third-person form meaning 'let (someone) call' or 'summon' (an instruction). 'آن خردمند را' is the definite object (marked by 'را') 'that sage', and 'نامدار' is a postpositive adjective 'renowned'. The whole segment is an order to have that wise man called.
segments.s354-g20: 'کز' is the relative 'who/that'. 'ایدر' = 'here/from here'. 'برو' is an imperative/subjunctive 'go' (used in a purpose clause). 'تا' introduces the purpose/result 'so that/that', and 'در شهریار' means 'into the (presence of the) king' (poetic use of 'در' with 'شهریار'). Together: 'who from here should go to the king.'
segments.s354-g21: 'بگویش' is an imperative 'tell him'. 'که‌گر' (که گر) combines 'that/if' to introduce a conditional clause. 'باژ ایران دهی' = 'you give/raise the cry of Iran' — here 'دهی' means 'to give/emit' (i.e. to shout the cry). The clause addresses the conditional action that follows the 'tell him'.
segments.s354-g22: 'به' here marks the manner/response 'in/at' or 'to' (i.e. in obedience). 'فرمان' is 'command' and 'گرایی' is a deverbal/nominal form meaning 'inclination/tendency (toward)'; together they convey 'obedience to the command'. The coordination 'و گردن نهی' pairs this with 'گردن نهی': 'گردن' = 'neck' and 'نهی' in this idiom functions as an archaic jussive of 'نهادن' ('to place/put') i.e. 'lower/submit the neck'. Thus the phrase gives the required response: obey the command and bow the neck. (The last listed sense for 'نهی' in the provided options does not match this classical usage, so its sense is given as -1.)
segments.s354-g23: 'به ایران' sets the domain/realm 'over Iran'. 'بماند' is an optative/jussive third-person singular 'may ... remain'. 'بتو' is the dative 'to you'. 'تاج و تخت' (crown and throne) are the things wished to remain with the addressee; poetic word order places them after the verb.
segments.s354-g24: 'جهاندار' is a title/noun 'ruler of the world' used here as a blessing; 'باشی' is second-person subjunctive/jussive 'may you be' (a wish or imperative). 'و پیروزبخت' appends 'and fortunate' modifying the same person: 'be sovereign and fortunate.'
segments.s354-g9: به + لهراسپ + گویم = 'I say to Lohrasp'. The preposition به precedes the addressee, and گویم is a first-person present/subjunctive form ‘I say / I shall say’ whose subject is incorporated in the verb (the independent pronoun ‘I’ is omitted). که introduces the subordinate content clause that follows; نیم جهان is the clause’s noun phrase complement 'half the world'.
segments.s354-g10: تو داری = 'you have/possess' (تو is the explicit subject, داری the present tense verb 'have'). به آرام is an adverbial/benefactive phrase ('with/ in tranquillity'); the coordinating و links another possession: گنج مهان is a noun + modifier/genitive construction 'treasure of the great' (مهان is a following noun that characterizes/possesses گنج). Word order is subject + verb + possessions joined by و.
segments.s354-g11: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if ...'. باژ is the object 'a cry/clamour' and بفرستی is the present-subjunctive/optative form of فرستادن used after اگر to express the conditional 'if you send'. از مرز خویش = 'from/out of your border': از + مرز (border) and خوی + ش (split in the wordlist) together produce the reflexive/possessive sense 'your own border'.
segments.s354-g12: ببینی is the apodosis verb (present/subjunctive used idiomatically for the consequence: 'you will see/you shall see'). سرمایه ارز خویش is a noun phrase: سرمایه 'capital/asset' modified by ارز 'value/wealth', and finished by خوی + ش giving the possessive 'your own' — noun + modifier + possessive clitic order typical in older Persian.
segments.s354-g13: بریشان here is a contracted/enclitic sequence meaning بر ایشان 'against them' (بر + ایشان) with بر governing the target. سپاهی is the object 'an army' and فرستم is first-person present/future 'I send'; ز روم is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from Rome' (ز = from). Word order places the target/agent phrases before the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s354-g14: که introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that'. از نعل پیدا is a prepositional/measure phrase literally 'from the horseshoe (از نعل) visible/apparent (پیدا)', with پیدا functioning adjectivally. نبینند is a negative third-person plural form 'they do not / will not see'; بوم 'homeland' is the clause’s object at the end (typical SOV ordering). The whole clause gives the intended effect of the sent army.
segments.s354-g15: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus gave an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد past tense 'gave', with پاسخ as the object — note the verb-before-object collocation). که introduces the reported speech or content clause that follows. این رای تست = 'this counsel/plan is yours' where این 'this', رای 'plan/counsel', and تست is an archaic/copular+possessive sequence functioning like 'is yours' (the wordlist lacks a direct 'is/are' sense for تست, so it is historically a contracted predication).
segments.s354-g16: زمانه = 'time/age/fortune' is the theme being spoken of. بزیر کف پای is a fixed prepositional phrase 'under the sole of the foot' (بزیر 'under', کف 'sole/palm', پای 'foot'), and تست again closes the clause with the meaning 'is yours / belongs to you' — a poetic/archaic predicative form rather than a lexical 'test' sense.
segments.s354-g97: This clause describes the subject. یکی is the indefinite 'a certain'; گرگ بد is a noun + adjective 'a bad/fierce wolf'; همچو introduces a simile 'like/as'; پیلی here is an adjectival comparison 'elephantine, like an elephant'; به دشت is a locative phrase 'in/on the plain' (به + place). Word order (determiner - noun - adjective - simile - place) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s354-g98: This relative clause qualifies the preceding wolf. که is the relative 'which/that'; قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar'; نیارست is an older/poetic verb form meaning 'did not (manage/withstand)' in context (an archaic or irregular negative past); زان سو گذشت is a phrase 'passed that way' (زان = 'from that/thence', سو = 'side/way', گذشت = 'passed'). The clause as a whole: 'which Caesar could not withstand, passed that way.'
segments.s354-g99: بیفگند is an older past-tense verb 'he hurled/threw down' (compound/poetic form). The conjunction و links two coordinated actions. دندان او را uses the object marker را to mark 'its/his tooth' as the direct object; بکند here is the second verb 'he tore/struck out' (an older/perspectival form of کردن used in narrative). The sequence is: 'he hurled (it) and (then) pulled out its tooth.'
segments.s354-g100: وزو is an archaic contraction meaning 'from that/therefrom' (essentially وز او = 'from him/thereof'); کشور روم = 'the realm/country of Rome'; شد is the past copula/auxiliary 'became'; بی‌گزند is a predicate adjective 'unharmed/without injury'. Together: 'and from that the Roman realm became unharmed.'
segments.s354-g101: بدو is the clitic/adverbial 'to him' (indirect object first, a typical older order). گفت is the verb 'said' (here used with an object clause); لهراسپ is the subject/name 'Lohrasp' placed after the verb (common in epic syntax). کای راست‌گوی is a vocative phrase: کای = 'O!' and راست‌گوی = 'truth-speaking/faithful one' addressing the addressee.
segments.s354-g102: کرا is an archaic interrogative word 'who (is it that)...' placed at the start; ماند is the past of مانَدَن 'remained/was left'; این مرد پرخاشجوی is the noun phrase 'this quarrelsome/aggressive man' (این = this, مرد = man/hero, پرخاشجوی = quarrelsome). The clause asks 'Who is this quarrelsome man who remains?' with the interrogative first.
segments.s354-g103: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative phrase 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = thus, داد = gave, پاسخ = reply/answer). The conjunction/particle که introduces the content of the answer. باری نخست is a temporal adverbial 'at first/once at the outset' (باری = once/one time, نخست = first/initially).
segments.s354-g104: به چهره is an instrumental/observational phrase 'by his face/by appearance' (به used adverbially 'by/through'); زریرست is a nominative identification with the archaic copula suffix -ست — 'he is Zarir'; گویی درست is a comment 'it seems true/indeed so' (گویی = 'it seems/one would say', درست = 'correct/true'). Together: 'By his face he is Zarir, it seems true.'
segments.s354-g65: به شگبیر marks the beneficiary with the preposition به ('to/for') and names the person (Shagbîr). قالوس شد uses شد ('became/was made') with قالوس as the (grammatical) subject — a passive/ resultative sense: 'a qalūs (palanquin/sedan) was made/appointed.' بارخواه is a compound of بار ('load') + خواه ('one who wants') functioning as an agent-noun 'porter' or 'one who seeks/handles loads', modifying or appositioning the person for whom the palanquin was made.
segments.s354-g66: ورا is the unstressed form of the third-person object pronoun 'to him/for him' and is the indirect object of راه دادند ('they gave [a] way' → idiomatically 'they made way/let him pass' or 'they led him'). نزدیک شاه is an adverbial phrase 'near the king' placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s354-g67: ز before a noun here introduces the relating circumstance (poetic use equivalent to 'with regard to' / 'concerning' or 'from') and attaches to بیگانه ایوان 'the stranger's iwan/porch'. بپرداختند is a transitive/perfective verb here meaning 'they attended to / they took care of / they provided for' (i.e. they saw to the stranger's reception at the iwan).
segments.s354-g68: فرستاده را: فرستاده 'messenger' marked with the object/definiteness marker را. پیش functions adverbially as 'in the presence of' (i.e. before the king). بنشاختند is the transitive perfect/pluperfect 'they set (him) to sit' / 'they seated (him)' (causative-sense: they caused him to sit down).
segments.s354-g69: بدو 'to him' is an indirect-object clitic-like prepositional pronoun placed before the verb. گفت here functions as the main speech verb 'said.' لهراسپ is the proper name (Lohrasp). کای is a vocative particle/title 'O Kay' addressing the king; پر خرد is an adjectival phrase 'full of wisdom' where پر 'full (of)' precedes the abstract noun خرد 'wisdom'.
segments.s354-g70: مبادا is a negative optative/precaution 'may it not be / lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause. جان is the subject 'life/soul', جز 'except' restricts what follows, خرد is 'wisdom', and پرورد (from پروردن) is the verb 'nurture/bring up' — here used in the optative/subjunctive sense following مبادا ('may it not be that life should nurture anything except wisdom').
segments.s354-g71: بپرسم is a first-person verb form (prefix بـ for subjunctive/future nuance) meaning 'I will ask' or 'let me ask.' ترا (here given as ت + را in the tokenization) is the second-person object 'you' + object marker. راست functions as 'straight/true' modifying پاسخ‌گزار, a compound noun 'answer-giver' meaning 'one who answers' — together 'give a straight/honest answer.'
segments.s354-g72: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' بخردی ('if [you are] wise') uses the notion of خرد/خردمندی — here conditional on the addressee's wisdom. کام کژی is a noun phrase 'crooked/wrong desire' (کام 'desire' modified by the following adjective کژی 'crookedness/wrongness'). مخار is a (poetic/archaic) negative-imperative/verb meaning roughly 'do not conceal / do not harbor / do not indulge' — so the clause counsels: 'if you are wise, do not harbor crooked desire.'
segments.s354-g57: بران گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner' (بران 'thereon/thereupon' + گونه 'manner, way'). بنواخت is a past-tense 3rd-person singular (classical form with the prefixبـ-) from نواختن/بناختن meaning 'played/struck'; او را is the definite object (او = 'him') marked by را. به بزم is a locative/prepositional phrase 'at/for the banquet' placed after the verb, so literal order: 'In that manner struck him at the feast.'
segments.s354-g58: تو گفتی is a past 2nd-person clause 'you said' (تو = 'you', گفتی = 'you said'); که introduces the reported content 'that'. نشنید is the simple past 3rd-person negative ('he did not hear' — ن- negation + شنید). پیغام رزم is the object of نشنید: 'the message (پیغام) of war/battle (رزم)'.
segments.s354-g59: شب آمد is a straightforward past clause 'night came/fell.' پر اندیشه پیچان is an adjectival phrase 'full of twisting/tangled thoughts' (پر = 'full (of)', اندیشه = 'thought', پیچان = 'twisting/tormenting') modifying the (implicit) subject. بخفت is past 3rd-person 'he slept' (classical form with بـ-). The clause order is topical: time — modifier — verb.
segments.s354-g60: تو گفتی که is the same reporting frame 'you said that' introducing the subordinate clause. با درد و غم is a prepositional phrase 'with pain and sorrow' (با = 'with'; درد here denotes emotional suffering). بود جفت is a copular past construction: بود = 'was' and جفت functions as predicate 'paired/joined (with)', so together 'he was paired with/attended by pain and sorrow.'
segments.s354-g61: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the golden throne' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', زرین = 'golden'). نشست is past 3rd-person 'sat'/'took its seat.' The clause is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو.
segments.s354-g62: شب تیره is the nominal subject phrase 'dark night' (تیره = 'dark/ominous'). رخسار خود را is the object phrase 'its own countenance/face' with خود reflexive/emphatic and را definite object marker. ببست is the past verb 'he/it closed/bound' used here idiomatically 'veiled/covered' — so 'dark night veiled its face.'
segments.s354-g63: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (classical register). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that'/'and thus'), and رفت پیشش is a verb phrase 'went before him' (رفت = 'he went', پیشش = 'into his presence/before him'). زریر is the proper name of the one who went. The sequence literally reads 'he commanded so that Zarrir went before him.'
segments.s354-g64: سخن گفت is a compound expression meaning 'he spoke' (سخن = 'speech', گفت used as the verbal element 'said/spoke'). هرگونه is an adverb 'in any manner/variously' modifying سخن گفت. با شاه دیر is the prepositional phrase 'with the (long‑reigning/aged) king' — دیر here gives the sense of remoteness/long time (i.e., old/long‑standing).
segments.s354-g105: This is a prepositional/locative phrase headed by the preposition به introducing a list of qualities (بالا, دیدار, فرهنگ, رای) joined by و. The nouns are enumerated in a compact, poetic order (no ezāfe); the whole phrase specifies the domains in which the following predicate applies.
segments.s354-g106: زریر is the subject; دلیرست is an adjectival predicate with the archaic copula‑ending -ست (= هست) meaning “is brave.” گویی here functions parenthetically as an evidential/compare‑like particle 'as if/it seems' introducing a simile; بجای is an adverbial phrase 'in place / in his proper position' qualifying the comparison. Together they yield 'Zarir is brave, as if in his place.'
segments.s354-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' (3rd person) and is followed by the subject لهراسپ (Lohrasp) in a poetic word order; بگشاد is a past verb 'opened' (here 'brightened/revealed') and چهر is its object 'face/countenance' — 'when Lohrasp heard, he opened/brightened his face.'
segments.s354-g108: بران is an adverbial/relational particle 'upon/at him' linking the action to the addressee; مرد رومی is the subject 'the Roman man'; بگسترد is the past verb 'spread/distributed' (here 'showed, extended'); مهر is the object 'kindness/favor' — 'the Roman man spread favor upon him.'
segments.s354-g109: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly/plentifully'; ورا is the enclitic object pronoun 'to him/onto him'; برده here is a past form from بردن ('took/gave' in poetic usage) and بدره is a poetic noun 'full moon' used metaphorically for a beautiful woman; داد is the past verb 'gave.' The phrase means he gave him many things and presented a fair one.
segments.s354-g110: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; درگاه 'the court/presence (of the ruler)' is the place; برگشت is the past verb 'he returned'; پیروز and شاد are predicative adjectives 'victorious and joyful' describing his state on returning.
segments.s354-g111: بدو is the clitic/phrase 'to him'; گفت is the past reporting verb 'said' introducing direct speech or a command; کاکنون is an adverb 'now'; به قیصر بگوی is an instruction 'tell (it) to Caesar' where بگوی is the hortative/subjunctive form used to express 'go and tell.'
segments.s354-g112: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of the reported speech; من is the subject 'I'; با سپاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with an army'; آمدم is the past tense 'I came'; جنگجوی is a predicate/occupational noun 'as a warrior' appended without a copula (typical poetic compression).

sentences.s354-s17: When he came near the great king, he saw that gate and the great palace.
sentences.s354-s18: When news reached the commander of the baggage, he came stately to the king.
sentences.s354-s19: For an old, world‑wise man rightly (knew this): verily he is Caesar’s messenger.
sentences.s354-s20: He was mounted — with him many renowned men — and he kept seeking the way to the king.
sentences.s354-s21: When he heard it, he sat upon the ivory throne and placed that heart-cheering crown upon his head.
sentences.s354-s22: The great men of Iran all sat before the throne, joyful of heart and fortunate.
sentences.s354-s23: He commanded, so they lifted the curtain; they sent the messenger away happily.
sentences.s354-s24: When he came near his throne, he praised him highly and led him to prayer.
sentences.s354-s41: The messenger said, “O spokesman of the king, I have come to the Khazar frontier as a toll‑collector.”
sentences.s354-s42: I suffered much in prophethood; never did anyone ask about this matter.
sentences.s354-s43: But the king so treated me that one should not bend the neck to crookedness.
sentences.s354-s44: A horseman came close to him who had seized a lion out of the thickets in his hand.
sentences.s354-s1: After this, too, some time passed; the heavens were inwardly troubled and did not show their face.
sentences.s354-s2: Vishtaspa then said to the warrior: 'So long as you live, seek (your gain) from this world.'
sentences.s354-s3: Ponder these words with wisdom, for thought lodged in speech is to be taken in (i.e. to be inwardly received).
sentences.s354-s4: I will send to Iran an envoy — a worldly-wise, pure, and noble one.
sentences.s354-s45: He laughs at men even on the day of battle, and at feasts he too wears a wine‑coloured garment.
sentences.s354-s46: At feasts and in battle and on the day of the hunt, the world has not seen a rider like him.
sentences.s354-s47: He gave him a more bountiful daughter, who was dearer than his helmet.
sentences.s354-s48: He became a token within Rome; when he turned into a male dragon, the vanquished fell into his grasp.
sentences.s354-s25: He delivered to the Caesar the precious message, just as ought to be done according to ritual and justice.
sentences.s354-s26: The king was filled with sorrow at his words; he grew angry at the turn of fate.
sentences.s354-s27: They adorned a precious place and made the envoy sit down happily.
sentences.s354-s28: They sent brocaded cloths for laying out, garments to wear, and also edibles.
sentences.s354-s37: These arts did not exist within Rome's inner court; the Caesar was abject before the kings.
sentences.s354-s38: Now he sent an extortioner to a district, and he set up a post.
sentences.s354-s39: When Elias — who was at the Khazar frontier — it was said he had glory and a quarrelsome temper.
sentences.s354-s40: He would seize and bind continually with an army — by this extortioner, who showed the way.
sentences.s354-s13: Otherwise they will fall on me with a mighty army — even spearmen from Rum and from the plains.
sentences.s354-s14: Watch out: an uproar may arise from the plain, with Farkhzād — their victorious leader — at the front.
sentences.s354-s15: I will utterly destroy all your homeland; by the sword I will make Iran a wilderness.
sentences.s354-s16: A messenger came like the wind; his head full of wisdom, his stern heart full of justice.
sentences.s354-s9: There was a renowned man named Qalous — wise and learned, endowed with counsel and desire.
sentences.s354-s10: Summon that renowned sage, so that he may go from here to the king.
sentences.s354-s11: Tell him that if he raises the cry of Iran, he should respond to the command and bow his neck (i.e. submit).
sentences.s354-s12: May the crown and throne of Iran remain with you; be sovereign and fortunate.
sentences.s354-s5: I will tell Lohrasp that you possess half the world, tranquillity, and the treasure of the great.
sentences.s354-s6: If you send a cry beyond your border, you will see the value of your capital (your own wealth).
sentences.s354-s7: I will send against them an army from Rome so that they will not see their homeland even by the sight of a horseshoe.
sentences.s354-s8: He answered thus: 'This plan is yours; time is under the sole of your foot.'
sentences.s354-s49: A certain fierce wolf, elephantine, in the plain — which the Caesar could not withstand — passed that way.
sentences.s354-s50: He hurled (it) and pulled out its tooth, and from that the realm of Rome became unharmed.
sentences.s354-s51: Lohrasp said to him, 'O truthful one, who is this quarrelsome man that remains? '
sentences.s354-s52: He replied thus: 'At first sight — by his face — he is Zarir, it seems indeed so.'
sentences.s354-s33: They set up a palanquin for Shagbîr, the porter, and made way for him close to the king.
sentences.s354-s34: They tended the stranger's iwan and seated the envoy before (the king).
sentences.s354-s35: He said to him, 'O Kay Lohrasp, full of wisdom — may it not be that life nurtures anything but wisdom.'
sentences.s354-s36: I will ask you; be a truthful answerer — if you are wise, do not conceal a crooked desire.
sentences.s354-s29: In that manner he struck him at the banquet; you said that he did not hear the message of war.
sentences.s354-s30: Night came; full of tangled thoughts he slept; you said that he was joined with pain and sorrow.
sentences.s354-s31: When the sun sat upon the golden throne, dark night veiled its own countenance.
sentences.s354-s32: He commanded, and Zarrir went before him; he spoke in every fashion with the aged king.
sentences.s354-s53: In rank, appearance, refinement and counsel, Zarir seemed brave, as if in his proper place.
sentences.s354-s54: When Lohrasp heard, he brightened his countenance; the Roman man then showed him favor.
sentences.s354-s55: He bestowed plentiful gifts upon him and gave a moonlike beauty; from the court he returned victorious and joyful.
sentences.s354-s56: He said to him, 'Now go and tell Caesar that I have come with an army as a warrior.'
